Hot water from the storage tank is cooled through the first heat exchanger, circulated to the use points, and then reheated in a second exchanger before returning to the storage tank. Sanitation of the loop is accomplished by turning off the cooling medium on a periodic basis. The major disadvantage is its high energy consumption, since it cools and reheats the circulating water regardless of whether it is drawn out of the loop.

7. Hot Tank, Self-Contained Distribution (Figure 8-9)

This configuration is most advantageous when water is generated hot, there are many low temperature water users, and energy consumption is critical. Hot water from the storage tank is cooled through the heat exchanger, circulated to the use points, and then returned to the pump suction bypassing the storage tank. The loop is sanitized on a periodic basis by turning off the cooling medium and opening up the return to the storage tank, allowing hot water to flow through the loop.

When water is drawn out of a point of use valve, hot water from the storage tank flows into the loop and is cooled by the heat exchanger. The hot water flushes the short section of line between the storage tank and the circulation pump preventing a deadleg.

8. Hot Storage, Hot Distribution, Point of Use Heat Exchanger (Figures 8-10, 8-11, 8-12)

This configuration is identical to Hot Storage/Hot Distribution except that use points requiring water at lower temperature are equipped with point of use heat exchangers. Three different designs are shown:

  • Figure 8-10: Single point of use, steamed for sanitization
  • Figure 8-11: Point of use installed in sub-loop (minimizes water waste)
  • Figure 8-12: Multiple branch users, flushed hot for sanitization

All three allow flushing water to drain to lower microbial counts and adjusting temperature before opening the use valve. Point of use exchangers are most advantageous when there are both hot and lower temperature water use points off the same loop, and the number of low temperature users is small. As the number of low temperature users increases, capital costs and space requirements become prohibitive. Water consumption is high due to flushing. Maintenance requirements are high due to the added exchangers and valves.

Commentary | 解說

系統架構實例（6-8）

6. 熱儲存 + 冷卻再加熱

高能耗 微生物控制極優，消毒最簡單（關掉冷卻水即可），但能源浪費嚴重 — 不管有沒有人用水，迴路都在持續冷卻 + 再加熱。

適用：微生物控制要求極嚴格、沒有時間消毒、多個低溫使用點、能源成本不是關鍵。

7. 熱槽 + 自含式配送

能源優化版 這是架構 6 的改良版 — 冷卻後的水回到泵浦吸入側而不是回到儲水槽，避免了持續再加熱的能耗。只有實際被取用的水才需要冷卻。

消毒方式：關閉冷卻水，打開回到儲水槽的管路，讓熱水沖洗整個迴路。

8. 使用點熱交換器

高維護成本 主迴路維持熱循環（最佳微生物控制），只在需要低溫水的使用點安裝個別冷卻器。每個冷卻器都需要獨立的沖洗和消毒程序。

適用：少量低溫使用點 + 大量高溫使用點

不適用：低溫使用點多（成本和空間爆炸）

練習思考：如何選擇？

你的工廠有以下需求，應該選哪種架構？

  • WFI 系統，水在 80°C 蒸餾產出
  • 20 個使用點，其中 15 個需要熱水 (65°C+)，5 個需要常溫 (25°C)
  • 能源成本是次要考量
  • 微生物控制是最高優先

參考答案：架構 8（使用點熱交換器）最適合 — 主迴路維持熱循環，只有 5 個低溫點需要冷卻器，數量不多，維護可控。

Original Text

8.3.5 Storage and Distribution Comparison Table (Table 8-1)

Table 8-1 compares several storage and distribution options currently used in the pharmaceutical industry. Comparisons are made based upon capital, energy, operating costs, maintenance, validatability, and other factors. Each category is rated Low (L), Medium (M), or High (H) for each system relative to the other systems presented.

Category 1. Batched 2. Branched 4. Hot/Hot 5. Amb/Amb 6. Cool & Reheat 7. Self-contained 8. POU HX
Capital Cost HLLL-MMMH
Water Use HHLL-MLMH
Energy LLLLHMM
Validatability SimpleComplexSimpleAverageSimpleAverageAverage
Operability ComplexComplexSimpleAverageAverageAverageAvg-Complex
Maintenance MLMLMMH
Microbial Risk L-MHLML-ML-MM
Peak Demand LimitedExcellentExcellentExcellentAverageAvg-ExclLimited

Notes:

  • (1) Lower microbial risk with hot water sanitization once every 24 hours
  • (2) Storage tank is always hot, loop is cold/ambient, hot water sanitized once every 24 hours
  • (3) Frequent hot water flushing or steaming can effectively control bioburden in branched systems

Configuration 9 (Tankless, Ambient Loop) is also listed: Capital L, Energy L, suitable when space constraints or tank turnover are concerns.

Commentary | 解說

8.3.5 配水系統比較表解讀

Table 8-1 是系統選型的「速查表」

這張表是本章最具實用價值的工具。設計時可以根據你最在意的因素（微生物控制、能源、資本）來快速篩選適合的架構。

關鍵觀察

  • 微生物控制最佳：#4 Hot/Hot（L）— 80°C 持續循環幾乎不給微生物機會
  • 資本成本最低：#2 Branched（L）和 #4 Hot/Hot（L）
  • 能源消耗最高：#6 Cool & Reheat（H）— 持續冷卻再加熱
  • 操作最簡單：#4 Hot/Hot — Simple 驗證 + Simple 操作
  • 水消耗最高：#1 Batched 和 #8 POU HX — 前者是排水換槽，後者是沖洗浪費
  • 維護成本最高：#8 POU HX（H）— 每個使用點都有獨立的換熱器和閥門

比喻：選車

選配水系統就像選車 — Hot/Hot 是 Toyota（可靠、簡單、法規認可），Cool & Reheat 是 BMW（性能好但油耗高），Branched 是機車（便宜但不安全），POU HX 是改裝車（靈活但維護費驚人）。沒有完美的系統，只有最適合你工況的系統。

練習：使用比較表

你的場景：新建 PW 系統，預算有限，水在常溫產出，使用量穩定但不大，有充足的消毒時間。該選哪個？

參考：#5 Ambient/Ambient — 資本成本 L-M，能源 L，操作 Average，只要有足夠消毒時間就能維持品質。

Original Text

8.4 Materials of Construction

Pharmaceutical equipment and piping systems rely extensively on stainless steels to provide the non-reactive, corrosion-resistant construction needed in manufacturing and heat sterilization. However, thermoplastics are available that may offer improved qualities, or lower cost.

Less expensive plastics such as polypropylene (PP) and polyvinyl chloride (PVC) may be acceptable for non-compendial systems. Others, such as polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) offering greater heat resistance, may be suitable for compendial waters, although they require continuous support in hot applications.

The cost of a PVDF system may be approximately 10-15 percent lower than the cost of a stainless steel system once factors such as passivation, boroscope, radiographic inspection, etc., are included. New methods of joining PVDF tubing leave a weld much smoother than possible with stainless steel.

At higher temperatures, however, thermal expansion of the plastic becomes a major concern.

Material selection should be consistent (all 316L or all 304L etc.) throughout the distribution, storage, and processing systems, if regular passivation is planned.

For compendial water, the use of 316L stainless steel is preferred.

Insulation for stainless piping should be free of chlorides, and hangers provided with isolators to preclude galvanic corrosion.

High purity water distribution systems should be joined using acceptable welding or other sanitary techniques. Orbital welding has become the preferred method for joining high purity metallic water piping systems, due to the greater control over critical weld parameters and the smooth weld bead characteristics of the process.

304 and 316 stainless steel have been preferred grades for use in metallic piping systems. Low carbon and low sulfur grades of stainless steel are preferred for compendial systems. A maximum sulfur content of 0.04% would be ideal for welding but any mismatch in the sulfur content of the mating parts will easily cause the weld to weaken.

Where possible, all fittings, valves, tubing, and weldable pieces of the same nominal size should be purchased and manufactured from steel with the same specification and Heat Number in order to standardize the weld quality for each tubing size.

Commentary | 解說

8.4 建造材料

材料選擇層級

  • 藥典水（Compendial）：316L SS 首選，PVDF 也可接受
  • 非藥典水（Non-compendial）：PP、PVC 可用（便宜得多）

316L SS vs. PVDF 比較

特性316L SSPVDF
成本（含安裝檢測）較高低 10-15%
焊接表面有凹凸更光滑
蒸汽消毒YesYes（需控溫控壓）
熱膨脹低高（需連續支撐）
Rouge 風險有無
法規接受度最高高（歐洲更常見）

焊接品質管理要點

  • Orbital welding 是高純水管路的首選接合方式
  • 同批號鋼材：相同尺寸的管件、閥門、管材應來自同一 Heat Number，避免硫含量不匹配導致焊接缺陷
  • 低碳低硫：compendial 系統必須使用低碳（L）等級
  • 硫含量：理想 max 0.04%，但兩端匹配比絕對值更重要
  • 材料一致性：整個系統用同一等級（全部 316L 或全部 304L），這對鈍化程序很重要

比喻：血管的材料

選擇管材就像選擇人工血管的材料 — 必須與體內環境（高純水）完全相容，不能有任何東西溶出或腐蝕。316L SS 就像是經過幾十年驗證的標準材料，PVDF 是新一代的替代方案，在某些方面（如防 rouge）甚至更好，但需要特別注意熱膨脹的問題。

Original Text

8.4.1 Comparison of Materials of Construction (Table 8-2)

Table 8-2 compares the relative values of key factors in the design and installation of water systems across different materials.

Factor PVDF ABS Polypro PVC 316L Tube 304L Tube 316L Pipe 304L Pipe
Installed CostMMLLMMMM
Ease of InstallHMMHMMHH
Steam SanitizableYNNNYYYY
Hot Water SanitizableYNNNYYYY
Ozone SanitizableYNNNYYYY
Chemical SanitizableYYYYYYYY
Rouge Suscept.NNNNYYYY
Corrosion Resist.HHHHHMHM
ExtractablesLMLHLLLL
Thermal ExpansionHHHN/ALLLL
External SupportHHHMLLLL

8.4.2 Workmanship

Fabrication should be performed by certified welders in a controlled environment to preclude contamination of equipment and material surfaces. Facilities dedicated to the fabrication of stainless steel are preferred, to avoid contamination by carbon steel. Fabrication must follow an approved quality assurance plan.

Tubing and piping welds, whether orbital or manual, must have a smooth internal diameter contour without excessive concavity or convexity, bead wandering, misalignment, porosity, or discoloration. One hundred percent photographic or radiographic analysis, while utilized to an increasing extent, is neither cost effective nor infallible. Appropriate sampling is strongly recommended.

Commentary | 解說

8.4.1-8.4.2 材料比較與施工品質

Table 8-2 關鍵解讀

  • PVDF 是唯一能蒸汽消毒的塑膠 — ABS、PP、PVC 都不行
  • PVC 的 extractables 最高 — 只適合非藥典水
  • 所有塑膠的熱膨脹都高 — 需要大量外部支撐
  • 所有不銹鋼都有 rouge 風險 — PVDF 完全沒有
  • 304L vs. 316L：316L 的耐蝕性更好（H vs. M）

施工品質管理重點

  • 認證焊工：必須在受控環境中操作
  • 專用不銹鋼加工區：避免碳鋼污染
  • 焊接品質標準：光滑內徑、無凹凸、無偏移、無孔隙、無變色
  • 100% 射線檢測？ ISPE 認為不是必要的，也不完美 — 建議適當的抽樣檢測即可

這是一個很實務的觀點 — 100% 檢測既昂貴又不能保證零缺陷，合理的抽樣計劃加上良好的製程控制更有效。

練習：材料選擇決策

場景：歐洲新建 PW 系統，使用臭氧消毒，預算有限，rouge 是客戶關切重點。

你會選什麼材料？為什麼？

參考：PVDF — 臭氧相容、無 rouge 風險、成本低 10-15%、歐洲接受度高。但必須注意熱膨脹（雖然臭氧系統通常在常溫運行，所以問題不大）。

Original Text

8.5 System Components

8.5.1 Heat Exchangers

Shell and tube, tube-in-tube, and plate and frame heat exchangers are employed. Although plate and frame units may offer a cost advantage, they are used less often in the distribution portion of the system in compendial service because of the perceived greater risk of contamination.

In a shell and tube exchanger treated water flows through the tube bundle; the risk of contamination from cooling or heating media can be significantly reduced by means of a double tube sheet. Complete drainability of the u-tube bundle is achieved by weep holes located at the low point of each chamber. Ensuring a positive pressure differential on the "clean" side can further reduce contamination risk.

8.5.2 Vent Filters

Used on storage tanks in compendial water service to reduce contamination during drawdown. Units are constructed of hydrophobic PTFE or PVDF to prevent wetting and generally rated at 0.1 to 0.2 microns. Filters should be capable of withstanding sterilization temperatures and sized for maximum fill or drawdown rates to effectively relieve the negative pressure created by high temperature sanitization cycles.

Filters in hot systems are usually jacketed to minimize condensate formation that could result in blinding vessel exhaust. Storage tanks should be rated for full vacuum, (or have vacuum protection), if steam is used for sterilization. Vent filters should be integrity tested for compendial water storage tanks, but may not need to be validated as sterile filters.

8.5.3 Pumps and Mechanical Seals

Centrifugal pumps are commonly employed in distribution systems. Performance curves and suction head requirements should be reviewed to preclude cavitation. The generation of pump heat over extended periods of low or no draw off should also be considered, since significant temperature rise in cold systems, or cavitation due to vapor pressure in hot systems could occur.

Although with double mechanical seals, with WFI or other compatible seal water flushing may minimize the possibility of contamination; single mechanical seals flushed to the outside have also been used. The installation of dual pumps, for standby purposes, should ensure flow throughout the system.

8.5.4 Piping System Components

  • Piping and Tubing: Extruded seamless and/or longitudinally welded tubing is commonly used in systems 2 inches and smaller. Welded steel tubing (ASTM A-270) has become available at significantly lower cost. PVDF has also been shown to be a viable alternative.
  • Fittings: Single fittings may be manufactured from as few as one, to as many as five pieces. This can materially affect suitability in terms of weld content, documentation, and cost.
  • Valves: The trend has been to use diaphragm valves in high purity systems, particularly in isolation applications. For steam service, sanitary ball valves may be acceptable.
Commentary | 解說

8.5 系統元件

熱交換器選型

類型優點缺點適用
Shell & Tube可用 double tube sheet 防污染體積大、成本高藥典水配送側
Tube-in-Tube低洩漏風險傳熱面積小小流量使用點
Plate & Frame成本低、效率高污染風險較高前處理側

關鍵原則：潔淨側（clean side）必須保持正壓，防止冷卻/加熱介質洩漏到純水側。

呼吸過濾器設計要點

  • 材質：疏水性 PTFE 或 PVDF，0.1-0.2 μm
  • 尺寸：必須能應對最大進水/抽水速率
  • 熱系統：必須加熱夾套防止結露阻塞
  • 蒸汽消毒：儲水槽必須能承受全真空或裝設真空保護
  • 完整性測試：藥典水必須做；但不需要驗證為滅菌級過濾器

泵浦設計考量

  • 離心泵是標準選擇
  • NPSH 計算：熱水系統必須考慮蒸氣壓
  • 泵浦發熱：低流量長時間運轉會導致冷系統升溫或熱系統氣蝕
  • 機械密封：雙機械密封 + WFI 沖洗（最安全）或單密封外排
  • 備用泵：確保系統不停流

閥門趨勢

高純水系統的趨勢是使用隔膜閥（diaphragm valve）：

  • 可排淨（drainable）
  • 可消毒
  • 無死角
  • 缺點：初始成本和維護成本較高，磨損較快

蒸汽服務則使用球閥（ball valve），因為隔膜在蒸汽下壽命短。

Original Text

8.5.5 System Components Comparison Table (Table 8-3)

The following is a summary of water system components, listing the common industry practice, and listing advantages and disadvantages.

Component Industry Practice Advantages Disadvantages
Valves Diaphragm; Plug/Ball; Butterfly Diaphragm: drainable, sanitizable, no steam seal, no body pockets. Ball: low cost, tight shutoff, low maintenance Diaphragm: higher cost & maintenance, wears out quicker. Ball: not absolute shutoff, stem seal, body pockets
Gaskets Elastomers (Viton, Silicone, EPDM, Teflon, Teflon-encapsulated) Each has different temperature/chemical resistance characteristics Chemical resistance varies; Teflon most inert but most expensive
Vent Filters 0.2μm hydrophobic membrane, steam jacketed or electric traced housing Bioburden & particulate reduction, sanitary design Possible plugging due to wetting, cost
Heat Exchangers Double tube sheet (S&T); Single tube sheet (S&T); Concentric pipe; Plate & Frame Double TS: protection against leaks. P&F: least expensive P&F: greatest leak potential, needs double gasketing. Concentric: low HT coefficient
Pumps Centrifugal; Positive Displacement Centrifugal: commonly available. PD: higher pressure, more efficient PD: non-shrouded impeller cleanability concern
Mech. Seals Double; Single Double: constantly flushed, higher reliability. Single: less expensive Double: more expensive, higher maintenance
Connections Sanitary clamped; Flanged Clamp: minimal crevicing, easy inspection/disassembly. Flange: good for high pressure Clamp: pressure/size limitations. Flange: gasket protrusion, crevicing
Tanks Jacketed (1/2 pipe); Jacketed (full); Non-Jacketed Full jacket: good thermal efficiency. 1/2 pipe: allows external inspection Full jacket: significant welding required. Non-jacketed: needs external HX
Commentary | 解說

8.5.5 元件比較表解讀

元件選型速查指南

這張表是日常工程設計的實用參考。以下是各元件的「最佳選擇」：

  • 閥門：高純水用隔膜閥，蒸汽用球閥
  • 墊片：Teflon 或 Teflon 包覆型最安全（惰性最好）
  • 呼吸過濾器：0.2μm 疏水膜 + 加熱夾套（熱系統必備）
  • 熱交換器：藥典水用 double tube sheet S&T，前處理用 P&F
  • 泵浦：離心泵為主，需要高壓時用正位移泵
  • 機械密封：WFI 用雙密封 + WFI 沖洗，PW 可用單密封
  • 連接：盡量用衛生夾具（Tri-Clamp），高壓才用法蘭

實務經驗分享

Table 8-3 中最容易被忽視的是儲水槽夾套設計：

  • 半管夾套（1/2 pipe jacket）：可以從外部完整檢查槽體焊縫，這在驗證時非常重要
  • 全夾套（full jacket）：熱效率好但焊接量大，而且槽體焊縫被遮蔽無法外部檢查
  • 現代趨勢傾向半管夾套，因為它平衡了熱效率和可檢查性
Original Text

8.6 Comparison of WFI Systems with and without Storage Tank

It is possible to feed sub-loops off a single main loop without the use of an intermediate storage tank (Figure 8-13). This configuration is most advantageous when space or capital constraints are tight. The sub-loop is generally a circulating loop. Water drawn out of the main loop when a point of use valve is opened cannot return to the main since the sub-loop is at a lower pressure. This provides a degree of isolation between the sub-loop and the main.

Table 8-4: Comparison of WFI Systems

Storage Tank SystemTankless System
Advantages
  • Provides air break to minimize back contamination of hot WFI supply
  • Minimize WFI cooler capacity by averaging hot WFI feed flow into tank
  • Provide cooled WFI "surge" volume ready on demand
  • Positive point to relieve system pressure due to normal venting or hot water sanitization
  • Once operational, conditions easier to maintain; potential problems easier to isolate
  • Eliminate back pressure control valve cavitation
  • Decreased capital expense (no tank, filter, etc.)
  • Increased perception of improved system sterility due to "totally welded tubing"
  • If drained daily, potential WFI lost may be less than storage tank system
Disadvantages
  • Added capital expense of tank, filter, etc.
  • Sanitization/steamout may be more involved
  • If drained daily, potential WFI loss may be greater
  • WFI tank may be required to satisfy peak demands of ambient system
  • Thermal expansion during hot water sanitization has nowhere to relieve, except flush to drain
  • More difficult to isolate than storage tank system
  • System hydraulics more difficult to manage/control
Commentary | 解說

8.6 有槽 vs. 無槽系統比較

核心差異

有儲水槽的系統提供了一個「緩衝區」和「壓力釋放點」，讓系統更穩定、更容易管理。無槽系統省了設備成本，但在操作和液壓控制上更具挑戰。

有儲水槽的關鍵優勢

  • Air break：防止 WFI 供水端的反向污染 — 這是衛生設計的重要概念
  • Surge volume：冷卻後的 WFI 儲備量可以即時供應生產需求高峰
  • 壓力釋放：消毒或正常通氣時有明確的壓力釋放點
  • 故障隔離：有問題時更容易定位和隔離

無槽系統的陷阱

  • 熱膨脹問題：熱水消毒時水的體積膨脹無處可去，只能排放到 drain — 浪費 WFI
  • 尖峰需求：沒有緩衝量，可能還是需要在主迴路上加槽
  • 液壓控制：無槽系統的壓力和流量管理更複雜

無槽系統的「全焊接管路」在觀感上似乎更無菌，但實際操作中，有槽系統通常更容易維持穩定的水質。

總結思考：你的工廠該選哪個？

大多數情況下，有儲水槽的系統是更安全的選擇。無槽系統只在以下場景值得考慮：

  • 空間極度受限
  • 資本預算非常緊張
  • 系統小且用水量穩定（無尖峰需求）
  • 有經驗豐富的工程團隊管理液壓

Section Summary | 章節總結

  • 儲水槽是配水系統的核心，提供緩衝、壓力釋放和微生物控制。設計時需考慮容量、位置、類型（vertical/horizontal）、spray ball、夾套加熱、呼吸過濾器等。
  • 8 種配水架構各有優劣 — Hot/Hot（#4）是法規最認可的黃金標準，Ambient/Ambient（#5）是最經濟的選擇，Cool & Reheat（#6）微生物控制好但能耗最高。
  • 風險導向設計是本章的核心哲學 — 不是堆疊所有設計功能，而是選擇 ROI 最高的功能組合。
  • 材料選擇：316L SS 是藥典水首選，PVDF 是有競爭力的替代方案（成本低 10-15%、無 rouge、焊接更光滑），但需注意熱膨脹。
  • Orbital welding 是高純水管路的首選接合方式。同批號鋼材（Heat Number 一致）對焊接品質至關重要。
  • 系統元件：離心泵 + 隔膜閥 + double tube sheet S&T 熱交換器 + 0.2μm 疏水呼吸過濾器是標準配置。
  • 有槽 vs. 無槽：大多數場景下有儲水槽的系統更穩定、更易管理。無槽系統只在空間/資本受限的特殊場景下考慮。
ISPE Baseline® Guide Volume 4: Water and Steam Systems | p137-p145

Learning Objectives

  1. Understand the fundamental conditions that aggravate microbial problems and the basic measures to counteract them in water systems.
  2. Master the regulatory clarifications on common industry practices: finishes, tank orientation, tank isolation, turnover, drainability, deadlegs, positive pressure, and loop velocity.
  3. Differentiate between continuous microbial control methods: hot systems (≥65°C), cold systems (4-10°C), and ambient systems with periodic sanitization.
  4. Evaluate the roles of ozone, UV light, microfiltration/ultrafiltration, and circulation as continuous control tools.
  5. Compare periodic sterilization/sanitization methods: chemical, ozone, and heat-based approaches.
  6. Apply system design principles for sanitization -- materials of construction and storage tank design considerations.

Section Contents

8.7 Microbial Control Design 8.7.1 Regulatory Clarification 8.8 Continuous Microbial Control 8.8.4 Ozone 8.8.5 UV Light 8.8.6 Filtration 8.8.7 Circulation 8.9 Periodic Sterilization 8.10 System Design
8.7 Microbial Control Design Considerations

Original Text

In any given water storage and distribution system, there are certain fundamental conditions that can always be expected to aggravate a microbial problem. Likewise, several basic measures will always tend to counteract such problems. Fundamental conditions that typically could aggravate the problem include:

  • Stagnant conditions and areas of low flow rates
  • Temperatures that promote microbial growth (15-55°C)
  • Poor quality supply water

Some basic measures that have been shown to alleviate such problems are:

  • Maintaining ozone levels of 0.02 ppm to 0.2 ppm
  • Continuous, turbulent flow
  • Elevated temperatures
  • Proper slope
  • Smooth, clean surfaces that minimize nutrient accumulation
  • Frequent draining, flushing, or sanitizing
  • Air breaks in drain piping
  • Ensuring no leaks in the system
  • Maintaining positive system pressure

All pharmaceutical water must meet the EPA standard for microbiological quality of potable water; which means it must basically be free of specific indicator organisms. Beyond that, microbiological quality for non-compendial water should be based upon its intended use and the types of products that will be formulated with it.

It is important to note though that although the required microbial population acceptance level for USP compendial purified water is 100 CFU/ml, reliance on such a single parameter can be misleading. The 100 CFU/ml limit may generally be applied to the manufacture of solid oral dosage forms. Many times, however, aqueous or topical formulations require tighter controls to maintain product quality. The USP points out that these types of products have been the subject of recalls when found to be contaminated with gram negative organisms, and the typical microbiological flora of water are gram negative organisms.

A common appropriate approach to dealing with this key issue involves the use of trend analysis. Using such an approach, alert and action levels are related to the system norm. In this context, strategies for responding to the alert and action levels can, and should, be developed. Even with the most conscientious design, there may be places in which biofilm can form. Good Engineering Practices, such as eliminating deadlegs, ensuring adequate flow velocities throughout the system, and periodic sanitization help control microbial activity.

It is common practice, therefore, to store and distribute water in a circulating system under any of the following scenarios:

  • At conditions which are self-sanitizing such as above 65°C or under ozone.
  • At conditions that limit microbial growth such as below 10°C with periodic sanitization.
  • At ambient temperatures where sanitization is determined by the validated methods that control microbial growth.

Tutorial Commentary

Core Concept -- Microbial Control Triad

Three enemies of water system microbial quality:

  • Stagnation (停滯): still water = bacteria paradise. No flow = no shear force to prevent attachment, nutrients accumulate on surfaces.
  • Temperature sweet spot (溫度甜蜜區 15-55°C): this is the "danger zone" for microbial growth. Most waterborne bacteria thrive in this range.
  • Poor feedwater (原水品質差): garbage in, garbage out. High bioburden in feedwater puts enormous pressure on downstream treatment.

The nine countermeasures listed in the original text are the engineering "toolkit" for fighting these three enemies. Every design decision in sections 8.7-8.10 maps back to these fundamentals.

Analogy

Think of your water system as a swimming pool. If you stop the circulation pump, let the water sit at room temperature, and skip chlorination -- what happens in a week? Algae blooms, murky water, bacteria everywhere. A pharmaceutical water system faces the same biological reality, just with much stricter standards. The nine countermeasures are like the pool's circulation pump + chlorine + filtration + regular cleaning -- you need a combination of continuous and periodic controls.

Key Notes

100 CFU/ml -- a misleading "safety net":

  • USP's 100 CFU/ml for Purified Water is an ACTION limit, not a design target. If your system routinely runs at 80-90 CFU/ml, you are already in trouble.
  • For liquid oral products, topicals, and aqueous formulations, FDA expects much tighter limits -- often 10 CFU/ml or lower as alert limits.
  • The key insight: use trend analysis (趨勢分析) to set YOUR system's alert and action limits based on actual capability, not just USP minimums.

Three Operating Strategies

StrategyConditionTypical ApplicationSanitization Need
Hot (熱循環)≥65°C (optimal 80°C)WFI systemsInherently self-sanitizing
Cold (冷循環)4-10°CBiotech, some PWReduced frequency
Ambient (常溫)15-30°CPW systemsFrequent (ozone or hot water)

As a CDMO, your choice of operating strategy determines lifecycle cost, energy consumption, and operational complexity. Hot systems are most reliable but most expensive to run. Ambient systems are cheapest but require the most vigilant sanitization programs.

8.7.1 Regulatory Clarification to Common Industry Practices

Original Text

The following are industry practices that are all Good Engineering Practices (GEPs), and have been perceived in the past to reduce the chance of microbial growth.

If you collectively ignore all of these items, you increase the likelihood of having a bioburden problem. These items include finishes, storage tank orientation, storage tank isolation, storage tank turnover, piping slope and drainability, deadlegs and velocity.

8.7.1.1 Finishes

Common industry practices typically range from milled pipe to 320 grit (0.38 microns Ra) mechanical polish with electropolish. Electropolishing is a reverse plating process, which improves the surface finish of mechanically polished stainless steel piping and equipment. It reduces surface area and removes surface intrusions caused by mechanical polishing, which may cause subsequent rouging, and/or discoloration. After mechanically polishing or electropolishing the system, the polishing compounds should be confirmed to have been completely removed from the pipe, so as not to accelerate corrosion.

The benefits for electropolish or finishes smoother than 0.76 microns Ra (approx. 180 grit or 30 micro inch) are questionable.

Systems operating at ambient temperature or with infrequent sanitization may require a smoother surface finish. The interior surfaces of stainless piping systems, in compendial water service, are typically ground and/or electropolished, at considerable cost, to achieve a smooth surface of minimal porosity (0.4 to 1.0 microns Ra), in order to reduce bacterial adhesion and enhance cleanability. A viable alternative is extruded PVDF piping, which can produce a smoother surface than most metallic systems, without recourse to polishing, although PVDF has other disadvantages.

8.7.1.2 Storage Tank Orientation

Vertical orientation is the most common because of the following advantages:

  • Lower fabrication cost
  • Less dead volume
  • Simpler spray ball design
  • Less floor space required
  • Horizontal vessels are used where height is a constraint

8.7.1.3 Storage Tank Isolation

Common practice for compendial and non-compendial waters where microbial contamination is a concern is to use a 0.2 micron hydrophobic vent filter.

For hot storage vessels, the vent filter must be heated to minimize moisture condensation. An alternate practice is to blanket the tank with 0.2 micron filtered air or nitrogen. Nitrogen blanketing can be used if CO2 absorption is a concern, or if final product oxidation is a problem.

8.7.1.4 Storage Tank Turnover

Common practice is 1-5 tank turnovers per hour.

The turnover rate may be important for systems using external sanitization or polishing equipment. The turnover rate is less important when storage is under sanitizing conditions, including hot storage or ozone. It may be less important under conditions that limit microbial growth, such as cold storage (4-10°C), but this must be demonstrated by documentation.

Some storage tank turnover is required to avoid dead areas.

8.7.1.5 System Drainability

Systems that will be steam sterilized must be fully drainable to assure complete condensate removal.

Systems that will never be steam sterilized do not need to be fully drainable, as long as water is not allowed to stagnate in the system.

It is good engineering practice to allow for the draining of equipment and associated piping.

Tutorial Commentary

Important Context

Why "Regulatory Clarification"? This section is ISPE explicitly saying: "These are GEPs, not absolute regulatory requirements." Many companies have over-interpreted these practices as FDA mandates. ISPE clarifies what is truly critical vs. what is helpful but not mandatory. This nuance is extremely valuable for CDMO engineers who must balance compliance with cost.

Surface Finish Comparison

FinishRa (microns)CostBenefit
Milled pipe (as-received)~1.6-3.2LowestAcceptable for non-critical
180 grit mechanical~0.76ModeratePractical minimum per ISPE
320 grit + EP~0.38HighGold standard for WFI
PVDF (extruded)<0.25ModerateSmoothest, but temp limited

ISPE's honest assessment: below 0.76 μm Ra, the incremental benefit is "questionable." This is a significant statement -- many companies spend enormous money on 320 grit + EP when 180 grit might suffice. The exception: ambient systems with infrequent sanitization genuinely benefit from smoother finishes.

Analogy -- Surface Finish

Surface finish in water pipes is like the smoothness of kitchen countertops. A rough wooden cutting board harbors bacteria in its grooves no matter how hard you scrub. A polished granite countertop is much easier to clean. But beyond a certain smoothness (like glass), the improvement becomes negligible -- and the cost skyrockets. Same logic applies to pipe finishes: there is a point of diminishing returns.

Tank Design Quick Reference

  • Orientation (方向): Vertical preferred. Less dead volume, easier spray ball coverage, lower cost. Horizontal only when ceiling height is a constraint.
  • Isolation (隔離): 0.2 μm hydrophobic vent filter is standard. For hot tanks, HEAT the filter to prevent condensation (wet filter = bacteria highway). Nitrogen blanket for CO2-sensitive or oxidation-sensitive products.
  • Turnover (周轉率): 1-5x per hour is the norm. Higher turnover = less stagnation = less biofilm risk. But under hot or ozonated storage, turnover is less critical because the storage conditions themselves are microbicidal.
  • Drainability (排水性): Steam-sterilized systems MUST be fully drainable (condensate traps). Non-steam systems: drainability is GEP, not absolute requirement, as long as no stagnation occurs.

CDMO Practical Tip

Turnover rate and client audits: Clients and auditors frequently ask "What is your tank turnover rate?" The correct answer is not just a number -- it depends on your operating mode. If your tank is continuously ozonated at 0.05 ppm, a turnover of 1x/hr is fine. If your tank is ambient without ozone, you need 3-5x/hr minimum. Always relate turnover to your microbial control strategy.

8.7.1.6 Deadlegs -- Critical Design Topic

Original Text

Good engineering practice is to minimize or eliminate deadlegs where possible. Common practice is to limit deadlegs to less than 6 branch pipe diameters or less. This stems from the "6D" rule contained in the proposed CFR 212 regulations of 1976. Recently, industry experts have suggested using a guideline of 3D or less. However, this new guideline causes confusion since the proponents of this standard generally are discussing the length of dead leg from the outer wall of the pipe, while the original 6D rule describes the distance from the center line of the pipe to the end of the deadleg. Obviously, if a 1/2" branch is placed on a 3" main, the distance from the center line of the pipe to the outer wall of the pipe is already 3D. Thus, even a zero deadleg valve might not meet the 3D requirement.

To avoid confusion in the future this Guide suggests that the length of the deadleg be considered from the outer wall of the pipe. We propose avoiding a hard rule of thumb for maximum allowable deadlegs.

Ultimately, the water must meet the required quality regardless of the length of the deadleg. Good Engineering Practice requires minimizing the length of deadlegs and there are many good instrument and valve designs available to do so.

It should be recognized that any one-way system can constitute a deadleg if it is not frequently flushed or sanitized.

8.7.1.7 Positive Pressure

It is important to maintain the system under positive pressure at all times. One common concern is systems designed with insufficient return flow, which could draw a vacuum at points of use under periods of high water usage. This causes an unexpected microbial challenge to the system.

8.7.1.8 Loop Velocity

Common practice is to design circulating loops for a minimum return velocity of 3 feet/second or higher, and for Reynolds numbers in the turbulent region of greater than 2,100.

Return velocities less than 3 feet/second are acceptable for short periods of time, or in systems that do not favor microbial growth, such as hot, chilled, or ozonated loops.

A minimum return flow is required to maintain the loop in a flooded condition under positive pressure.

Tutorial Commentary

CRITICAL: The Deadleg Debate -- 6D vs 3D

This is one of the most debated topics in pharmaceutical water system design. Understanding the nuance here can save you millions in unnecessary engineering and help you defend your design during audits.

  • 6D Rule (1976 proposed CFR 212): Measured from the CENTER LINE of the main pipe to the end of the branch. This was never finalized into law -- it was a proposed regulation that was withdrawn.
  • 3D Rule (modern industry): Measured from the OUTER WALL of the main pipe. Sounds stricter, but it is actually roughly equivalent to the 6D center-line measurement for typical pipe sizes.
  • The confusion: A 1/2" branch on a 3" main has 3D just from center-line to outer wall. So a "zero-length deadleg valve" already fails the 3D center-line test!

ISPE's resolution: Measure from the outer wall. But more importantly, don't treat any "D" number as an absolute rule. Focus on whether the water meets quality specifications.

Analogy -- Deadlegs

A deadleg in a water system is like a dead-end alley in a city. Main streets have constant traffic (flow) that keeps them clean. But the dead-end alley has no through-traffic -- garbage accumulates, puddles form, and it becomes unsanitary. The shorter the alley, the better, because some turbulence from the main street still reaches in. But beyond a certain length, the alley becomes completely stagnant. Your design goal is to keep all "alleys" as short as possible -- or better yet, eliminate them entirely by using through-flow designs.

Deadleg Measurement -- Visual Guide

Example: 1/2" branch on 3" main pipe

Measurement MethodStarting Point"Already used" distancePractical impact
6D (old, center-line)Center of main1.5" (= 3D of branch)3D of branch left for valve
3D (new, outer wall)Outer wall of main0"Full 1.5" for valve

ISPE's recommendation to use outer wall measurement resolves the mathematical absurdity of the center-line approach for large main / small branch combinations.

Positive Pressure & Velocity -- Key Parameters

  • Positive pressure (正壓維持): If the system drops to negative pressure during peak usage, ambient air (with all its microorganisms) gets sucked in through any tiny leak. This is an "uncontrolled ingress" event -- essentially defeating all your upstream treatment.
  • 3 ft/sec minimum return velocity (最低回流速度): This provides turbulent flow (Re > 2,100) that creates shear forces on pipe walls, discouraging biofilm attachment. Below 3 ft/sec, laminar flow allows bacteria to settle and attach.
  • Exception: Hot (≥65°C), cold (≤10°C), or ozonated systems can tolerate lower velocities for short periods because the microbial control comes from temperature or chemistry, not just flow.

Practice Questions

  1. Your system has a 2" branch on a 6" main. Calculate the deadleg distance under both the 6D (center-line) and 3D (outer-wall) rules. Which method gives more practical design space?
  2. During peak production, your loop return velocity drops to 1.5 ft/sec for 30 minutes. Is this acceptable? What factors determine the answer?
  3. An auditor asks: "Why don't you meet the 3D deadleg rule at point-of-use #7?" How do you respond? (Hint: ISPE's position on "hard rules of thumb.")
  4. Your CDMO has a one-way branch feeding a filling machine. This branch is 15 feet long with no recirculation. How do you address the deadleg concern?
8.8 Continuous Microbial Control

Original Text

Process water systems generally employ both continuous methods of microbial control, and periodic sanitizations. This section discusses continuous methods for controlling microbial growth.

8.8.1 "Hot" Systems

The most effective and reliable means of preventing the growth of bacteria is to operate the system at temperatures above which bacteria can survive. If the distribution system is maintained in hot conditions, sanitization on a routine basis can be eliminated.

Systems operating at 80°C have a long history of data showing the prevention of microbial growth. More recently, companies have been validating water systems at 65°C. The advantages of operating at lower temperature include energy savings, lower risk of injury, and reducing the amount of rouging. Systems operating at the higher end of this range have a greater safety margin with regard to microbial contamination. The effectiveness of temperatures below 80°C must be verified with test data, on a case by case basis.

Note that these temperature ranges will not destroy endotoxin. As noted in Chapter 6, where endotoxin is a concern, the treatment system must be designed to remove it.

8.8.2 "Cold" Systems

The use of the term "cold" in this case implies that a system is maintained at a low enough temperature to inhibit microbial growth. While this has been shown to be effective, the energy costs associated with it generally make this type system costly to operate. Generally, "Cold" systems are operated from 4°C to 10°C.

Microbial growth rates drop off significantly below 15°C, so the sanitization frequency of cold systems may be reduced compared to ambient systems. The effectiveness of a specific temperature, and the associated sanitization frequency in any particular system, must be determined by statistical analysis, on a case by case basis.

8.8.3 "Ambient" Systems

Circulation temperatures of any pharmaceutical water system are dictated by either the required microbial specification or the required temperature for usage. "Ambient" temperature purified water systems using either ozone and/or hot water sanitizations are common throughout the industry, and normally result in lower lifecycle costs, as well as reduced energy consumption compared with either the "hot" or "cold" systems.

However, without increased levels of system sanitizations, the lack of temperature control at the water storage vessel and distribution loop could result in the formation of a biofilm within the system, which could sporadically and unpredictably produce water failing microbiological specifications and necessitate non-scheduled water system shutdowns.

Tutorial Commentary

Hot vs Cold vs Ambient -- Decision Matrix

FactorHot (≥65°C)Cold (4-10°C)Ambient
Capital costHigh (insulation, HX)High (chillers)Lowest
Operating costHigh (energy)High (energy)Lowest (base)
Sanitization needNone (self-sanitizing)Reduced frequencyFrequent required
Microbial safety marginHighestGoodLowest (highest risk)
Operator safetyBurn riskLow riskLow risk
Rouging riskHigherLowerLowest
Endotoxin controlNO -- must remove upstreamNONO

Key Insight -- 80°C vs 65°C

The 65°C trend: Many modern facilities are validating at 65°C instead of the traditional 80°C. The benefits are significant:

  • ~20% energy savings
  • Reduced rouging (iron oxide formation accelerates with temperature)
  • Lower burn injury risk for operators
  • Less thermal stress on gaskets and instruments

But: 65°C has a smaller safety margin. You MUST validate with YOUR system's data. Some thermophilic organisms can survive at 60-65°C. ISPE explicitly says "the effectiveness of temperatures below 80°C must be verified with test data, on a case by case basis."

Analogy -- Three Temperature Strategies

Think of food storage. A hot buffet keeps food above 65°C to prevent bacterial growth -- it is inherently safe but energy-intensive (like hot water systems). A refrigerator at 4°C slows bacteria dramatically but does not kill them -- you still need to clean it periodically (like cold water systems). Leaving food at room temperature is cheapest but most risky -- you must eat it quickly or it spoils (like ambient water systems needing frequent sanitization). The "right" answer depends on your risk tolerance, budget, and usage pattern.

Biofilm Warning

The ambient system trap: Ambient systems look attractive on paper (lowest cost), but the biofilm risk is real and serious. Once biofilm establishes in a system:

  • It becomes extremely difficult to remove (protected by extracellular polymeric substances / EPS)
  • It sporadically releases planktonic bacteria, causing unpredictable test failures
  • It can resist sanitization agents at standard concentrations
  • Unscheduled shutdowns for remediation are far more costly than the energy savings from not running hot

For a CDMO producing injectable products, the risk calculus almost always favors hot systems for WFI loops.

8.8.4-8.8.7 Ozone, UV Light, Filtration, and Circulation

Original Text

8.8.4 Ozone

Ozone has been shown effective for microbial control. It is a strong oxidant, which chemically reacts with organisms and destroys them. The destruction of these organisms results in organic compounds, which may be further degraded by ozone, ultimately to carbon dioxide. Ozone is twice as powerful an oxidant as chlorine and needs to be dosed continually to maintain concentration.

In any compendial water system and most other applications, water at the use points is expected to be totally free of ozone. Ozone removal is commonly effected through ultra violet radiation. 254 nanometer UV light converts ozone to oxygen. A common design is to maintain an ozone concentration between 0.02 ppm and 0.1 ppm in the storage tank, and use a UV light at the beginning of the distribution loop for removal. To sanitize the loop itself, the UV light can be turned off during periods of no use, and the ozone will circulate through the loop. The UV dosage required for ozone destruction is generally 2 to 3 times that required for microbial control. Testing should be done to verify absence of ozone at the use points.

8.8.5 UV Light

Ultraviolet lights have been shown to reduce microbial populations in storage and distribution systems. UV energy is germicidal in the wavelengths of 200 to 300 nanometers, which falls below the visible spectrum. UV light de-activates DNA leading to bacteria reduction. A UV light is not a sterilization device, as it is often referred to. The effectiveness of the light will depend on the quality of the water in which it is acting, the intensity of the light, flow rate of water, contact time, and the type of bacteria present.

8.8.6 Filtration

Along with other particulate matter, bacteria and endotoxins may be removed via filtration. This filtration media can be either of the microfiltration (2-0.07 microns) or ultrafiltration (0.1-0.005 micron) scale. The integrity of these filters must be maintained.

8.8.6.1 Microfiltration

Microfiltration includes the use of depth cartridge filters, pleated filters, and cross flow filtration membrane elements. These filters can remove particles ranging in size from 100 microns down to 0.1 micron. Depth and pleated filters allow water to flow through a wall of fibers perpendicular to the water direction (dead ended filters). The particles are then trapped on the outside wall of these filters, or within the filter walls (for depth filters), due to the pore size of the filter. The filter will fill up with these particles and then needs to be replaced with a new filter.

8.8.6.2 Ultrafiltration

Ultrafiltration can be used to remove organics and bacteria, as well as most viruses and pyrogens from a water source. Filtration is typically from 0.1 micron down to 0.01 micron. Cross flow ultrafiltration forces the water to flow parallel to the filter media, and the particles which are too large to pass through the membrane elements are then expelled from the system in a concentrate stream to drain (typically 5-10% of the feed flow). This allows the filters to be self-cleaning and eliminates the need to replace these membrane elements frequently. This type of filtration can be used as a "maintenance" step downstream of the storage tank in certain situations.

In general, for any purified water system, filtration downstream of the storage tank is not recommended. This comes from concerns of "grow through" where bacteria will colonize on the upstream side of the filter, and ultimately be found on the downstream side even though the pore size of the media may theoretically be smaller than the size of the bacteria. An additional concern is the potential for accumulation of nutrients on the filter media, which may in fact increase the opportunity for microbial growth. However, filters downstream of a circulation pump are sometimes used in water systems. System designs should be predicated on obtaining the desired water quality upstream of the storage tank, using the treatment train. Filters downstream of the storage tank should not be relied on to purify the water.

8.8.7 Circulation

Most new water systems use a circulating loop for distribution. The primary purpose of circulation is to reduce the chance of microbial growth, or microbial attachment to the surfaces of the system. Although the mechanisms are not universally agreed upon, it is thought that the shear forces associated with turbulent water flow inhibit nutrient concentration and attachment of bacteria to surfaces. The velocity required to obtain these benefits is generally agreed to be greater than 3 feet per second, or Reynolds numbers greater than 2,100.

Velocity may drop off for short periods of time during high use times without adversely affecting the system, so long as positive pressure is maintained in the system. Circulation also serves to maintain proper temperature throughout the system in hot and cold systems.

Studies have shown that the velocities required to remove biofilm are higher than practical for a water system (above 15 ft/sec). However, a combination of high velocity (5 ft/sec or greater) with an antimicrobial agent, such as ozone or chlorine, may, over a long enough period of time, effectively remove biofilm.

A turbulent condition may be maintained in short dead ended pipe stubs if the length of the stubs is limited. This limiting length varies with the pipe stub diameter and to a lesser degree with the main pipe diameter. A rule of thumb for the maximum dead leg is 6 branch pipe diameters. This "rule of thumb" may be difficult to achieve in large mains with small branches, and may result in unacceptably long dead legs in large branches. Rather than universally applying "rules of thumb", it is important to recognize dead legs as an area of concern and take appropriate steps to prevent them in the original design or implement special provisions to address them if unavoidable.

Tutorial Commentary

Ozone System Design -- The Elegant Dual-Purpose Architecture

The ozone + UV design described here is one of the most elegant solutions in water system engineering:

  • Storage tank: Maintained at 0.02-0.1 ppm ozone -- continuously sanitized.
  • UV at loop inlet: Destroys ozone before distribution -- use points get ozone-free water.
  • Off-hours loop sanitization: Simply turn OFF the UV light, and ozone circulates through the entire loop.

Key design note: UV dosage for ozone destruction (2-3x) is much higher than for germicidal action. Size UV units for ozone destruction, not just microbial kill.

UV Light -- Common Misconception

"UV sterilizes water" -- WRONG. ISPE explicitly states UV is NOT a sterilization device. UV reduces microbial populations but cannot guarantee sterility. Its effectiveness depends on:

  • Water quality (turbidity reduces penetration)
  • UV intensity (lamp aging reduces output)
  • Flow rate and contact time
  • Bacteria species (some are more UV-resistant)

In audits, never claim UV "sterilizes" your water. Say it "reduces microbial populations" or "provides continuous microbial control."

Filtration Summary

TypePore SizeRemovesFlow ModeKey Concern
Microfiltration (MF)0.07-2 μmBacteria, particlesDead-end (perpendicular)Replacement frequency
Ultrafiltration (UF)0.005-0.1 μmBacteria, viruses, pyrogensCross-flow (parallel)5-10% reject to drain

CRITICAL: "Grow Through" -- Why Post-Tank Filtration is Risky

This is a counterintuitive but critical concept:

  • A 0.2 μm filter should theoretically block all bacteria (≥0.3 μm). In practice, bacteria can colonize the upstream side of the filter, forming biofilm.
  • Over time, bacteria can migrate THROUGH the filter pores ("grow through") via morphological changes or by exploiting micro-defects.
  • The filter surface becomes a nutrient-rich environment, actually PROMOTING microbial growth instead of preventing it.

ISPE's clear message: Design your treatment train to produce compliant water BEFORE the storage tank. Never rely on post-tank filtration as a "safety net."

Circulation -- Velocity Tiers

  • ≥3 ft/sec (Re > 2,100): Standard minimum. Turbulent flow prevents bacterial attachment. This is the design target for return loops.
  • ≥5 ft/sec + antimicrobial agent: Can gradually remove established biofilm over time. Use during remediation campaigns.
  • ≥15 ft/sec: Mechanical biofilm removal by shear force alone. Impractical for routine operation (excessive pump energy, erosion).

Key takeaway: Once biofilm is established, flow alone cannot remove it at practical velocities. Prevention is far easier than remediation. This is why the combination of flow velocity + chemical/thermal control is essential.

Practice Questions

  1. Your ambient PW system uses ozone in the tank and UV at the loop inlet. A user reports ozone odor at a distant use point. What might have gone wrong? How do you investigate?
  2. You discover biofilm in a section of your distribution loop. The loop velocity is 4 ft/sec. Why wasn't the velocity sufficient to prevent biofilm? What combination approach would you use to remediate?
  3. Your QA team wants to add a 0.2 μm final filter at each point of use "for extra safety." Using ISPE's guidance, how do you argue for or against this proposal?
8.9 Periodic Sterilization/Sanitization

Original Text

Periodic sanitization of storage and distribution systems is generally required. Based on monitoring the microbial quality of the system, a required frequency of sanitization should be formally established. Sanitization may also be done in response to reaching an "action limit" during routine testing. Various methods of periodic sanitization are discussed below.

8.9.1 Chemical

Various chemicals or combinations of chemicals can be used to periodically sanitize storage and distribution systems. Chlorine solutions on the order of magnitude of 100 ppm are very effective at killing organisms, but are not generally used in distribution systems because of corrosion problems associated with stainless steels. Hydrogen peroxide in concentrations on the order of 5% is a more practical alternative. Peracetic acid can also be used, generally in concentrations of 1% or less. Many different mixtures of these and other chemicals are commercially available for the purpose of sanitization.

Verification of the removal of the sanitizing agent is critical. Commercially available indicators (test strips or sticks) are commonly used to indicate when the amount of rinse water is sufficient. A rinse water analysis is then required to verify the absence of objectionable chemicals before the system is placed into service.

8.9.2 Ozone

Sanitation can be done either periodically or continuously with ozone. Storage tanks are typically continuously ozonated, and then the ozone is removed prior to the distribution loop or individual use points through the use of ultraviolet radiation. The distribution system can be periodically sanitized by turning off the UV light and, if necessary, increasing the ozone concentration while allowing it to circulate through the distribution loop. Concentrations as high as 1 ppm may be needed for periodic sanitization, particularly if biofilms must be removed.

8.9.3 Heat

It has been found that periodic sanitization by heating of the process water system is highly reliable and effective. The frequency at which sanitization must occur will vary depending on many factors:

  • System design
  • Size of distribution system
  • Components of system
  • Volume of process water in the system
  • Frequency of use of the process water (turnover volume)
  • Temperature of the circulating process water

Each distribution system must develop its own microbial profile, and the sanitization cycle and frequency will have to be developed to fit that system.

The most straightforward method of sanitization is to heat the circulating process water in the distribution system to 80°C ± 3°C and hold it at that temperature for a validated period of time. The use of this heat sanitization has been proven to be very effective and if designed properly can also be economical. Controls needed to perform this cycle of sanitization can be either manual or automatic.

Because of the types of bacteria found in purified water systems, the use of steam is not required for effective microbial kill. Steam sterilization of distribution piping may require additional valving for vents and drains, and may require a higher pressure rating than otherwise needed. Storage tanks are by their nature more easily steam sterilized and this practice is common, although not required.

Hot systems inherently are continuously sanitized. Thus, the need for sanitization should be based on microbial testing results, or when the system is off line for an extended period of time and the temperature of the loop drops to below the validated temperature range.

Depending on the process water specification, a conservative initial sanitization frequency should be assigned for "cold" systems. After the operating characteristics of the system are determined through microbial testing, the routine sanitization frequency can be determined.

8.9.4 Initial Sanitization (Ambient System)

Steam sanitization has a successful history, and is probably the most reliable method for sanitization. However, there is no requirement for steam sanitization in Purified Water or WFI systems. The following procedure is suggested as one option for hot water sanitization of an ambient system.

Immediately after passivation (for a SS system), the system should be flushed with process water at a high temperature (80°C ± 5°C) and all valves opened and points of use flushed. Normally two (2) times the volume of the system (after conductivity readings), or rinse water tests indicate that no passivation chemicals are detected, is required. This is the initial sanitization of the system.

Once it has been determined that the chemical characteristics for the quality of the process water have been achieved by USP chemical testing, then microbial samples should be taken after each component, the points of use, and the storage tank. This initial sampling should show that the distribution system at any sampling point has no viable bacterial contamination. Once this is achieved, the system should be brought down to its operating temperature and allowed to stabilize.

Tutorial Commentary

Sanitization Methods Comparison

MethodEffectivenessKey AdvantageKey Limitation
Chemical (H2O2, peracetic)HighNo heat requiredMust verify complete removal
Chlorine (100 ppm)Very highCheapest, most effectiveCorrodes 316L SS -- rarely used
Ozone (up to 1 ppm)HighDual continuous/periodic useHigher concentration for biofilm
Hot water (80°C ± 3°C)Very highNo chemical residuesThermal expansion, energy
SteamHighestMost reliable killOver-engineered for PW; extra valving

Key Insight -- "Steam is NOT Required"

This is a statement many people miss. ISPE explicitly says:

  • Steam sterilization is NOT required for PW or WFI distribution piping.
  • 80°C hot water sanitization is proven effective against waterborne bacteria in purified water systems.
  • Steam sterilization adds cost: higher pressure ratings, additional vent/drain valves, more complex piping design.
  • Steam IS common for storage tanks (easier to apply), but even that is "not required."

Practical implication: If your CDMO is building a new PW system, designing for hot water sanitization at 80°C instead of steam can significantly reduce capital costs without compromising microbial control.

Heat Sanitization -- Key Parameters

  • Target temperature: 80°C ± 3°C (validated hold time)
  • Frequency: Established per system microbial profile -- no universal rule
  • Trigger: Scheduled (preventive) OR reactive (action limit exceedance)
  • Automation: Can be manual or automatic; automatic preferred for consistency
  • Cold point validation: Must demonstrate 80°C is achieved at the coldest point in the system (often the furthest use point)

Analogy -- Sanitization Frequency

Sanitization frequency is like the cleaning schedule for a kitchen. A busy restaurant kitchen that handles raw meat (= ambient system under heavy use) needs cleaning after every shift. A kitchen that only reheats pre-cooked food (= hot system) needs far less frequent deep cleaning. And you cannot just copy your neighbor's cleaning schedule -- it depends on YOUR specific kitchen layout, menu, volume, and health inspection history. The same applies to water systems: each system develops its own microbial profile, and the sanitization program must be tailored to it.

Initial Sanitization -- Step-by-Step

For a new ambient SS system, the startup sequence is:

  1. Passivation -- chemical treatment to form the protective chromium oxide layer on stainless steel.
  2. Hot water flush (80°C ± 5°C) -- flush entire system including all points of use. Minimum 2x system volume.
  3. Conductivity verification -- confirm no passivation chemicals remain.
  4. USP chemical testing -- confirm water meets chemical specifications.
  5. Microbial sampling -- sample after each component, every POU, and the tank. Target: ZERO viable contamination.
  6. Cool down -- bring to operating temperature and stabilize.

Only after step 5 confirms zero contamination should the system enter routine operation. This initial sanitization establishes the "baseline" from which your trend analysis begins.

Practice Questions

  1. Your ambient PW system is sanitized with hot water weekly. After 6 months of data, microbial counts are consistently <5 CFU/ml. Can you reduce sanitization to biweekly? What process do you follow?
  2. After chemical sanitization with peracetic acid, your rinse water TOC is elevated. What is happening and how do you resolve it?
  3. An auditor asks why you do not steam-sterilize your PW distribution piping. Prepare your response using ISPE's guidance.
8.10 System Design for Sterilization/Sanitization

Original Text

8.10.1 Materials of Construction

The sanitization methods used must be compatible with the materials in the system. By far the most widely used material for storage tanks and piping is 300 series stainless steel (generally 316L). This choice provides the most flexibility with regard to sanitization options. Sanitization with heat, UV, or ozone can be used in stainless systems practically without restriction. Chemical sanitization must be carefully managed with regard to concentration, pH, and temperature to avoid corrosive effects on stainless distribution systems.

Other material used for distribution piping is PVDF. PVDF is susceptible to degradation by UV light. It is common to use stainless piping immediately adjacent to the UV light in a PVDF system to compensate for this problem. The temperature limitation of PVDF is approximately 140°C, which is high enough to allow heat sanitization or sterilization.

In stainless systems, the gaskets used must be reviewed for compatibility with the sanitization method. A widely used gasket material is PTFE or EPDM, both of which have good thermal memory and excellent resistance to temperature, ozone, and chemical sanitizers. Other gasket materials must be carefully reviewed for compatibility with the sanitization methods, and to ensure that they will not leach substances into the water.

The key is to recognize that the materials of construction "shall not be reactive, additive or absorptive so as to alter the safety, identity, strength, quality, or purity of the drug product beyond the official or other established requirements" (21 CFR 211.65). The sanitization procedures must be considered when selecting materials to comply with this requirement.

8.10.2 Storage Tank Design

Storage tanks are an area in the system that could be considered at high risk for microbial contamination because of the large surface area, low velocities, the need for venting, and potential for "cold spots" in the head space.

Tank size is generally based on economic considerations in combination with the pretreatment train sizing. From a bacterial standpoint, smaller tanks are preferred because they have higher turnover rates, which reduce the likelihood of bacterial growth. They also reduce surface areas and make it easier for ozone to permeate the water, if the tank is ozonated.

Spray balls may be used on the return loop to wet the head space of storage tanks. The use of a spray ball serves to keep the top of the tank at the same temperature as the water, in heated systems, and avoids alternately wet and dry surfaces, which could promote corrosive action with stainless steel and allow microbial growth. Connections on the top head (relief devices, instrument connections, etc.) should be kept as close to the head as possible to simplify the spray ball design and get the benefit of the spray action. An exception is the vent filter, which should be removed far enough from the storage tank to avoid direct contact from the water spray, which could blind the filter. If dip tubes or instruments project down from the head, multiple spray balls may be needed to avoid a "shadow" being created in the spray pattern.

The tanks must be vented to allow filling, and a filter should be used at the vent to avoid airborne particulate and microbial contamination. To avoid the problem of condensation in the filter and the resultant potential for colonization and grow through, hydrophobic vent filters are used and/or the filters are maintained at a temperature above the tank temperature with steam jacketing or electric tracing.

To help avoid microbial growth, and avoid the change in conductivity resulting from absorption of atmospheric gasses into the water, nitrogen blanketing on the head space may be used. This eliminates outside air passing into the tank through the vent filter. Note that gasses added to storage tanks should be appropriately filtered to avoid objectionable contamination.

Tutorial Commentary

Materials of Construction -- Decision Guide

MaterialHeatUVOzoneChemicalNotes
316L SSExcellentExcellentExcellentCaution neededGold standard, most flexible
PVDFGood (≤140°C)POOR (degrades)ExcellentExcellentNeed SS near UV units
PTFE gasketsExcellentGoodExcellentExcellentBest all-around gasket
EPDM gasketsGoodGoodGoodGoodGood thermal memory

21 CFR 211.65 reminder: Materials must not be "reactive, additive, or absorptive." This means your sanitization method cannot cause the materials to leach substances into the water. Chemical sanitization is the riskiest in this regard -- you must control concentration, pH, temperature, and contact time.

Storage Tank -- The "High Risk Zone"

ISPE identifies storage tanks as high-risk for microbial contamination. Four reasons:

  1. Large surface area: More surface = more potential biofilm attachment sites.
  2. Low velocities: Unlike the loop (3+ ft/sec), water in the tank moves slowly -- bacteria can settle.
  3. Venting requirement: The vent filter is the one point where the system communicates with the ambient environment.
  4. Head space cold spots: In hot systems, the tank head space above the water line may be cooler than the water, creating condensation that harbors bacteria.

Design principle: Smaller tanks are microbiologically superior. Size the tank for operational needs, not "just in case" capacity.

Spray Ball Design -- Critical Details

  • Purpose: Wet the entire head space to prevent alternating wet/dry surfaces (which promote both corrosion and microbial growth).
  • Temperature benefit: In hot systems, spray keeps head space at operating temperature -- no cold spots for condensation.
  • Top connections: Keep relief devices, instruments as close to the tank head as possible -- they benefit from spray coverage.
  • Vent filter exception: Keep the vent filter AWAY from spray action. Water on the vent filter can blind (block) it, and a wet filter is a microbial colonization site.
  • Shadow effect: If dip tubes or instruments protrude downward from the head, they can block spray coverage, creating unwetted "shadow" zones. Solution: multiple spray balls or 360-degree coverage designs.

Analogy -- Tank Head Space

The head space of a storage tank is like the ceiling area of a shower room. Even though the shower (spray ball) keeps the walls wet, the ceiling often has cold spots where condensation forms. Over time, mold (= biofilm) grows on those damp, cool surfaces. In a pharmaceutical tank, the spray ball is your "shower head" keeping the ceiling wet and warm. But you must be strategic about placement -- you want spray coverage everywhere EXCEPT the vent filter (which must stay dry to function properly).

Vent Filter & Nitrogen Blanketing

Two complementary strategies for tank isolation:

  • Hydrophobic vent filter: Allows air exchange during fill/drain cycles while blocking particles and microorganisms. "Hydrophobic" means it repels water -- critical because a wet filter promotes "grow through."
  • Heat the filter: Steam jacketing or electric tracing keeps filter above tank temperature -- prevents condensation inside the filter housing.
  • Nitrogen blanketing: Eliminates air exchange entirely. Benefits: no airborne contamination risk, no CO2 absorption (which lowers conductivity and pH). The nitrogen itself must be filtered (0.2 μm) before entering the tank.

For CDMO operations: Nitrogen blanketing is particularly valuable for WFI tanks and for products sensitive to oxidation. The cost of filtered nitrogen is modest compared to the microbial risk reduction.

Practice Questions

  1. Your facility is considering switching from 316L SS piping to PVDF for a new ambient PW loop. The loop has UV lights for ozone destruction. What design modification is needed near the UV units?
  2. During a routine inspection, you find the vent filter on your WFI tank is wet (water droplets visible inside the housing). What are the immediate risks? What corrective actions do you take?
  3. Your WFI tank is 10,000L but typical daily usage is only 2,000L. From a microbial perspective, what is the problem? What solutions would you consider?
  4. An engineer proposes using silicone gaskets in the distribution system because they are cheaper than PTFE. What concerns should you raise, referencing 21 CFR 211.65?

Section Summary

  • Three enemies of microbial quality: stagnation, temperatures between 15-55°C, and poor feedwater. All design decisions in this chapter aim to counteract these fundamental risks.
  • GEP vs regulatory mandate: ISPE clarifies that finishes, tank orientation, turnover, drainability, and deadleg rules are Good Engineering Practices, NOT absolute regulatory requirements. Collective compliance reduces risk; individual deviations may be acceptable if justified.
  • Deadleg measurement: ISPE resolves the 6D/3D confusion by recommending measurement from the outer wall of the main pipe. No hard rule of thumb -- focus on water quality outcomes.
  • Three operating strategies: Hot (≥65°C, self-sanitizing), Cold (4-10°C, reduced sanitization), and Ambient (cheapest but highest risk, requiring frequent sanitization). Each must be validated system-specifically.
  • Continuous microbial control tools: Ozone (0.02-0.1 ppm in tank, UV removal at loop), UV light (reduces, does NOT sterilize), filtration (MF/UF upstream of tank, not downstream due to "grow through"), and circulation (≥3 ft/sec turbulent flow).
  • Periodic sanitization: Chemical (H2O2, peracetic acid), ozone (up to 1 ppm for biofilm), and heat (80°C ± 3°C, most reliable). Steam is NOT required for PW/WFI systems.
  • Materials compatibility: 316L SS is the most versatile; PVDF degrades under UV. All materials must comply with 21 CFR 211.65 (non-reactive, non-additive, non-absorptive).
  • Storage tank design: Smaller tanks are microbiologically preferred. Spray balls keep head space wet/warm. Vent filters must be hydrophobic and heated. Nitrogen blanketing eliminates airborne contamination risk.
9

Chapter 9: Instrumentation & Control

儀控與自動化 | p147-p156

ISPE Baseline® Guide Volume 4: Water and Steam Systems | p147-p156

本章學習目標

  • 理解製藥水系統中儀器儀表的三大功能：控制設備運行、監控關鍵設備效能、記錄水質數據
  • 掌握 Critical vs Non-Critical Instruments 的定義邏輯 -- 影響最終水質的參數 = 關鍵參數，其對應儀器需要確認 (qualification)
  • 學會各類線上儀器的選擇與安裝要點：Conductivity（導電度）、TOC、pH、Ozone、Flow、Temperature、Pressure、Level
  • 理解導電度測量中溫度補償 vs 非補償的差異，以及為何 USP 要求最終品質測試必須使用非補償值
  • 掌握 Design Condition、Allowable Operating Range、Normal Operating Range 三層架構及 Alert/Action Level 的運用
  • 認識 Instrumentation Spikes（儀器尖峰）的成因與處理策略
  • 了解控制系統的四個自動化等級，以及控制軟體驗證與 21 CFR Part 11 的關聯

本章目錄 Chapter Contents

9.1 Introduction 9.2 Principles 9.3 General Requirements 9.3.1 Selection & Installation 9.3.2 Calibration 9.3.3 Types of Instrumentation 9.4 Design vs Operating Range 9.5 Instrumentation Spikes 9.6 Control Systems
9.1 Introduction

原文內容 Original Text

Instrumentation and controls are often used within pharmaceutical water systems to:

  • Control the operation of equipment and components
  • Monitor and document the performance of critical equipment
  • Monitor and document pharmaceutical water quality

The concepts and regulatory philosophy of defining critical versus non-critical parameters is discussed as it relates to instrumentation and controls. This definition could be summarized as:

"All instruments and control systems should be commissioned following Good Engineering Practices. Critical instruments and control systems should be commissioned and qualified."

There is no regulatory requirement that requires the use of On-Line instrumentation. A monitoring program may include a combination of On-Line instrumentation, manual documentation, and laboratory analysis.

If On-Line instrumentation is used to measure or record a critical parameter, action and alert limits may be established. The methods of addressing "spikes" are also discussed.

Automation can have a significant impact on the cost and performance of a pharmaceutical water system. There is no single optimum level of instrumentation and control for all systems. The optimum level for a given system balances the benefits of improved process control, improved documentation, and lower labor costs against the cost of procuring, installing, validating, and maintaining the instruments and control systems. In many cases, the level of automation for a pharmaceutical water system should be consistent with that utilized for the manufacturing process it supports.

導師解析 Tutorial Commentary

核心概念解析 Key Concepts

儀器儀表的三重角色：在製藥水系統中，儀器不只是「看數字」的工具，而是同時扮演三個角色：

  • Controller（控制者）：自動控制閥門、泵浦、加熱器等設備的運行
  • Monitor（監控者）：持續監控關鍵設備的效能，確保在規格內運作
  • Documenter（記錄者）：產生水質和運行數據的電子或紙本記錄，作為合規證據

Critical vs Non-Critical 的分界線：ISPE 的邏輯很清楚 -- 如果一個參數會影響最終水質，它就是 critical parameter，對應的儀器就需要 qualification（確認）。反之，只需按 GEP 進行 commissioning 即可。這個分類決定了你的驗證範圍和維護成本。

比喻說明 Analogy

把水系統的儀器想成醫院裡的監測設備。ICU 裡的心電圖監視器（critical instrument）必須定期校正、有報警功能、數據要完整記錄 -- 因為它直接關係到病人的生命安全。但候診室的溫度計（non-critical instrument）只要大概準確就好，不需要同等級的校正程序。製藥水系統也是一樣 -- 不是每個儀器都需要同樣嚴格的驗證，關鍵在於它測量的參數是否影響最終水質。

重點提示 Key Notes

沒有法規強制要求線上監測！這是很多人的誤解。ISPE 明確指出，監測方案可以是線上儀器、手動記錄、實驗室分析的組合。但在實務上，現代系統幾乎都會採用線上監測，原因有三：

  • 連續數據比抽樣更能捕捉到瞬態偏移 (transient excursions)
  • 減少人工介入，降低人為錯誤風險
  • 符合 data integrity 的期望 -- 電子記錄比手抄數據更具可追溯性

在 CDMO 環境中，客戶審計時看到完整的線上趨勢圖，信心會遠高於看到手抄的每日取樣記錄。

練習思考 Practice Questions

  1. 你的水系統有 50 個儀器點位，你會如何決定哪些是 critical、哪些是 non-critical？請列出你的判斷標準。
  2. 如果管理層為了節省驗證成本，要求你把所有儀器都歸類為 non-critical，你會如何回應？
  3. 為什麼 ISPE 建議水系統的自動化程度應與其所支持的製程一致？在 CDMO 中這意味著什麼？
9.2 Principles

原文內容 Original Text

a) To achieve GMP compliance, the manufacturer must demonstrate, through documented evidence, that the pharmaceutical water system is in control and consistently produces and delivers water of acceptable quality.

b) Although many quality attributes can be continuously monitored using On-Line instrumentation, there is no compendial or regulatory requirement for On-Line monitoring of pharmaceutical water quality. A monitoring program typically includes a combination of On-Line instrumentation, manual documentation of operational parameters, and laboratory analysis of water samples.

c) Instruments and control systems are critical and must be qualified when they are used to measure, monitor, control, or record a critical process parameter. A critical parameter is a processing parameter that affects the final water quality.

For example, the temperature of the final water product may be considered critical for microbial control. In this case, the temperature controls (e.g., sensors and alarms) would be considered critical. However, it is not necessary to consider the temperature of the heating media (e.g., steam) as a critical parameter.

Documentation should clearly indicate which instruments are critical and which are not. It is also advisable to identify non-critical instruments as such on the field device.

d) All instruments and control systems should be designed, installed, calibrated, and maintained appropriately, according to Good Engineering Practice. All critical instruments and controls require qualification.

e) Items that should be recorded in the system documentation include maintenance procedures and maintenance work performed, procedures for sampling and analysis, reporting the results, and trend analysis of the laboratory data. The monitoring program during start-up typically defines maintenance frequency and alert and action levels for the process variables.

導師解析 Tutorial Commentary

核心概念解析 Key Concepts

Critical Parameter 的判定邏輯：

  • 問自己一個問題：「這個參數如果偏離了，最終水質會受影響嗎？」
  • 如果答案是 Yes → Critical Parameter → 對應儀器需要 Qualification
  • 如果答案是 No → Non-Critical → GEP Commissioning 即可

ISPE 的例子很經典：產品水的溫度（直接影響微生物控制）= Critical。加熱蒸汽的溫度（間接影響，且有中間緩衝）= Non-Critical。這個邏輯可以推廣到所有儀器的分類。

重點提示 Key Notes

「在現場設備上標示 non-critical」這個建議非常實用。為什麼？因為：

  • 維護人員在現場看到儀器時，立即知道它的校正等級和頻率要求
  • 審計人員走訪現場時，可以快速確認儀器分類是否與文件一致
  • 避免「寧可多做」的心態導致不必要的校正工作，浪費資源

建議的做法：在每個儀器上貼標籤，註明 Critical / Non-Critical、校正到期日、校正頻率。

CDMO 應用場景 Application

趨勢分析 (Trend Analysis) 在 CDMO 中的價值：

ISPE 提到 start-up 期間的監測計畫要定義 alert/action levels。在 CDMO 環境中，趨勢分析的價值更大：

  • 客戶審計時會要求看過去 6-12 個月的水質趨勢圖
  • 趨勢的「斜率」比單次讀數更重要 -- 緩慢上升的導電度可能代表膜效能衰退
  • Alert level 的設定應基於歷史數據的統計分析，而非隨意設定

練習思考 Practice Questions

  1. 你的配液槽上有一個溫度感測器，用來監控混合過程中的溫度。這個感測器是 critical 還是 non-critical？為什麼？
  2. ISPE 說「沒有法規要求線上監測」，但你的客戶堅持要求所有 POU 都有線上導電度監測。你會如何評估這個要求的合理性？
9.3 General Instrumentation Requirements
9.3.1 Instrument Selection and Installation

原文內容 Original Text

a) Instruments should be selected for accuracy and reliability over the entire process range.

b) Instruments should be selected and installed in a way that reduces the potential for contamination.

  • Water contact surfaces should be constructed of materials that are compatible with the water they contact. Materials of construction and surface finishes are commonly specified for instruments installed in distribution systems.
  • Sensors in direct contact with waters with strict microbial limits should be of sanitary design. Non-sanitary instrumentation is commonly used in feed water and pretreatment systems.
  • Instruments may be installed directly in the water system or in a side stream that may, or may not, be returned to the main system.
  • Deadlegs should be avoided.

c) When possible, instruments should be installed such that exposure to harsh process conditions, such as pH and temperature extremes, is avoided. For example, In-Line sensors used to monitor effluent from a deionizer should be positioned such that exposure to regeneration chemicals is avoided.

Instruments that are not compatible with passivation agents, sanitization agents, or sanitization temperatures should be installed so that they may be easily removed or bypassed. Such devices may need to be sanitized off line.

d) Instruments should be installed in accordance with manufacturers' requirements to ensure proper operation. For example, flow meters should be installed in the proper orientation and with the correct upstream and downstream straight run of pipe. The impact of process and ambient conditions on an instrument's accuracy and reliability should be addressed.

e) Conductivity cells are especially sensitive to the presence of air or steam bubbles, which can be present where there is turbulence, cavitation, or flashing. Such locations should be avoided.

f) Accessibility for maintenance should be considered, but improving control response is usually more critical. Poor response time may be a consequence of the poor placement of a device and, in most cases, can be improved by installing the device closer to the point of measurement.

導師解析 Tutorial Commentary

核心概念解析 Key Concepts

六大安裝原則總整理：

原則關鍵要點
精度與可靠度選型要覆蓋整個製程範圍，不只是正常操作點
防污染Sanitary design、相容材質、避免 deadleg
耐環境避開極端 pH、溫度區域；不相容的儀器要可拆卸
依原廠規格安裝流量計的直管段要求、感測器方向等
導電度特殊考量避開氣泡、湍流、閃蒸區域
回應速度 > 維護便利安裝位置優先考慮測量準確性，而非維修方便

比喻說明 Analogy

想像你在裝家裡的煙霧偵測器。你會把它裝在廚房爐灶正上方嗎？不會，因為那裡天天「誤報」（就像把導電度探頭裝在湍流區會有氣泡干擾）。你也不會把它裝在車庫最角落「方便維修但感應不到」的位置（就像為了維護方便而把感測器裝得離測量點太遠）。最佳位置是走廊天花板 -- 離臥室近（回應快），又不會被日常活動干擾（環境適合）。

重點提示 Key Notes

Side Stream（旁路）安裝的優缺點：

  • 優點：不需要打開主管路即可維護/校正儀器；可以安裝不相容 sanitization 的儀器（如 pH 探頭）；不增加主管路的 deadleg
  • 缺點：測量值可能與主管路有時間延遲；需要控制旁路流速以確保測量準確；旁路本身需要定期沖洗或排放
  • 關鍵決策：旁路的水是否回到主系統？如果含有 pH 探頭的緩衝液洩漏風險，通常排放到 drain 而非回收

常見錯誤 Common Mistakes

Deadleg 的隱藏陷阱：很多人只注意管路上的 deadleg，卻忽略了儀器安裝本身可能製造的 deadleg。例如：

  • 溫度計套管 (thermowell) 插入深度不當
  • 壓力表連接管未保持最短距離
  • 液位計在儲槽上的接管 (stub) 過長

每一個「突出」都可能成為微生物的溫床，尤其在非持續流動的位置。

9.3.2 Instrument Calibration

原文內容 Original Text

a) The calibration of critical instruments should follow a regular program, which provides evidence of consistently acceptable performance. Non-critical instruments may be calibrated on a frequency deemed appropriate for the service.

b) Calibration should follow approved procedures and the results should be documented. Each component in a control loop should be calibrated individually or the loop may be calibrated in its entirety. All calibrations should be traceable to certified standards (e.g., NIST).

c) Vendor-supplied calibration certificates should reference the applicable instrument serial numbers. The impact of shipment and installation on the vendor's calibration should be addressed.

導師解析 Tutorial Commentary

核心概念解析 Key Concepts

校正的三個關鍵原則：

  • 頻率分級：Critical instruments = 定期校正（通常 6-12 個月）；Non-critical = 依服務需求決定（可以更長）
  • 可追溯性 (Traceability)：所有校正必須可追溯到認證標準（如 NIST），形成不間斷的校正鏈
  • 迴路校正 (Loop Calibration)：可以逐一校正迴路中的每個元件，也可以整個迴路一起校正。後者更能反映系統實際精度

重點提示 Key Notes

出廠校正 vs 安裝後校正：ISPE 特別提醒，供應商提供的校正證書可能因運輸和安裝而失效。實務上：

  • 精密儀器（如導電度計、TOC 分析儀）安裝後一定要重新校正
  • 校正證書上的序號必須與現場設備的序號一一對應 -- 這是審計時的常見查核點
  • 如果運輸過程中有衝擊或溫度極端，應額外進行功能測試

在 Data Integrity 的框架下，校正記錄也是 GMP 文件的一部分，必須完整保存且防止竄改。

9.3.3 Types of Instrumentation

9.3.3.1 Conductivity (導電度)

原文內容 Original Text
